topic: OKRs: Powerful framework or corporate theater?

turns.speaker: Lenny Rachitsky
turns.text: Welcome to The Arena, I am so pumped you're here for this one. On one side, Ben Horowitz — co-founder of a16z, author of The Hard Thing About Hard Things, and a founder who has actually had to run a company under fire, not just advise one. On the other side, Jason Cohen — founder of WP Engine, someone who has built and scaled a SaaS business from zero to hundreds of millions in revenue and written more honestly about startup operating systems than almost anyone I know. The question at the heart of this debate: do OKRs actually make companies better, or do they just make people feel like they do? Ben, I want to start with you — do OKRs work?

turns.speaker: Ben Horowitz
turns.text:
Look, this is actually a question I care deeply about, because the answer determines whether your company gets better or just gets better at *looking* like it's getting better.

Here's my core position: OKRs are a tool, and like any tool, they're only as good as the cultural foundation underneath them. As I talked about on Lenny's podcast, if you think about organizational design, goals and objectives, OKRs — any management technique — you need a basic underlying foundation for it to work. Without that foundation, OKRs don't become a powerful framework. They become exactly what the skeptics say they are: corporate theater. You're running a system that produces documents, not results.

When I was at Loudcloud and then Opsware, the existential question was never "did we hit our key results?" The question was "are we solving the right problem before we run out of money?" That clarity of purpose — that's what drives performance. What I've seen happen in a lot of companies, especially as they scale, is that OKRs become a substitute for that clarity rather than an expression of it. People spend enormous energy negotiating numbers, sandbagging targets, and then celebrating hitting 70% of a mediocre objective. That's not a management system. That's a performance. The *real* work of leadership is creating the context — the culture, the judgment, the shared understanding of what actually matters — so that when you do install a framework like OKRs, it amplifies something real rather than manufacturing something fake.

So here's the challenge I'll put on the table: before you debate whether OKRs work, ask yourself whether your company has the cultural operating system to run them honestly. Because if your people can't tell you bad news, if your managers can't make hard calls about priorities, OKRs won't fix that — they'll hide it.

sources.title: $46B of hard truths from Ben Horowitz: Why founders fail and why you need to run toward fear (a16z co-founder)
sources.snippet: Yeah. So we do a lot of things on that. So the kind of design of the firm is about confidence. So the first thing is, well, what would give

turns.speaker: Jason Cohen
turns.text:
Here's the thing about OKRs — the question itself is almost a trick, because the answer is *both*, and which one you get depends entirely on whether you've understood what problem you're actually trying to solve before you implement the framework.

The companies that turn OKRs into corporate theater do so in a predictable way. They set objectives that are really just restatements of their existing roadmap. They attach key results that are either so easy they'll hit them regardless, or so vague that "success" becomes a matter of interpretation at review time. Then everybody fills out the spreadsheet, the CEO nods approvingly, and nothing actually changes. As I talked about on Lenny's podcast, the thing about growth — or any metric, really — is that when things are going well, you focus on operational scaling, and when things slow down, suddenly everyone declares an emergency. OKRs set up the same dysfunction: you're essentially reacting to a stalled quarter by retroactively claiming your key results were always pointing that direction. That's not alignment. That's theater with extra steps.

Where OKRs become genuinely powerful is when they force a company to make *explicit tradeoffs* that were previously implicit and therefore invisible. The objective shouldn't describe what you're doing — it should describe *why that thing matters more than the other things you chose not to do*. That's the discipline most companies skip. So my challenge to anyone defending OKRs as implemented in the wild: can you point to a real decision your team *didn't* make — a project you killed, a priority you dropped — because of your OKR structure? If you can't name the thing you said no to, you probably have a prioritization document dressed up as a framework, not the real thing.

sources.title: 5 questions to ask when your product stops growing | Jason Cohen (2x unicorn founder)
sources.snippet: Jason Cohen: It clearly is more growth. It's just maybe a little more evocative, because if growth is good, yeah, sure, you want to grow mor

turns.speaker: Lenny Rachitsky
turns.text: Jason, I want to push back on something. You said the answer is "both" — that OKRs work or don't work depending on whether you've understood the problem first. But that's kind of a get-out-of-jail-free card, isn't it? By that logic, any framework works if you implement it correctly. So what's actually distinctive about OKRs that makes them worth the overhead versus just... good management?

turns.speaker: Ben Horowitz
turns.text:
Jason makes a point I actually agree with — the "thing you said no to" test is the right diagnostic. If your OKR process never produces a killed project or a dropped priority, you don't have alignment, you have documentation. But here's where I'd push back: the problem Jason is describing isn't a problem with OKRs, it's a problem with what I'd call the "eighth grade" issue I referenced on Lenny's podcast. You need a basic foundation before any management technique works. OKRs don't create intellectual honesty in a culture that doesn't have it. Nothing does. So blaming OKRs for theater is like blaming the playbook when the team won't run the plays.

The real failure mode I've watched up close — and I saw this with multiple companies we worked with at a16z — is that founders treat OKRs as a *communication* tool when they should be treating them as a *forcing function*. There's a massive difference. A communication tool tells people what you decided. A forcing function makes you actually decide. The companies that get value from OKRs are the ones where the CEO is willing to sit in a room and say "we cannot do both of these things, which one are we choosing" — and then live with the answer for a quarter even when it gets uncomfortable. Most companies won't do that. Most CEOs will find a way to keep both on the list because saying no to one feels like admitting the other one matters less, which feels like conflict.

So here's my challenge back: Jason's framing suggests we need a better framework than OKRs to enforce tradeoff discipline. But what framework actually solves the underlying problem, which is a CEO who won't make hard calls? Because in my experience, the bottleneck is almost never the framework. It's the person at the top who wants alignment without confrontation — and no system fixes that.

sources.title: $46B of hard truths from Ben Horowitz: Why founders fail and why you need to run toward fear (a16z co-founder)
sources.snippet: Yeah. So we do a lot of things on that. So the kind of design of the firm is about confidence. So the first thing is, well, what would give

turns.speaker: Jason Cohen
turns.text:
Ben, you're making my argument for me. When you say "no system fixes a CEO who won't make hard calls" — exactly right. So then the question becomes: why are we spending so much organizational energy implementing, training on, cascading, and reviewing OKRs, when the bottleneck you've correctly identified is a leadership behavior that OKRs cannot change? That's not blaming the playbook. That's asking whether you needed a playbook at all when the real work is coaching the quarterback.

Here's what I actually saw at WP Engine as we scaled: the periods where we got the most strategic clarity weren't when we had the most rigorous OKR process. They were when we were forced — by genuine resource constraints, by a competitor move, by a customer crisis — to have the "we literally cannot do both" conversation that you're describing. The forcing function wasn't the framework. It was reality. OKRs, in my experience, have a tendency to *substitute* for that conversation rather than *cause* it, because they give everyone the feeling of alignment without requiring the CEO to stand up and say "this one, not that one" in a way that someone in the room will be visibly disappointed by. That disappointment is the signal that a real decision got made. OKR documents rarely produce that signal.

So I'll take your challenge seriously: what actually works instead? I'd argue it's fewer, longer bets — what I think of as the "one thing" discipline — combined with radical transparency about *why* you're not doing the other things. Not a framework. A habit. A rhythm where leadership is constantly narrating the tradeoffs out loud, so the organization internalizes the logic rather than just the list. The challenge I'd throw back at you: if OKRs are supposed to be a forcing function, why do so many well-managed companies with strong CEOs still find themselves in Q3 realizing half their OKRs were aspirational noise from day one?

sources.title: 5 questions to ask when your product stops growing | Jason Cohen (2x unicorn founder)
sources.snippet: Lenny Rachitsky: Okay. I'm going to now take us to Contrarian Corner. The question here is, what's something that you believe that most othe

turns.speaker: Lenny Rachitsky
turns.text: Here's my hot take that neither of you has touched: OKRs might actually be most dangerous at exactly the moment companies think they need them most — the hypergrowth phase. Because when you're scaling fast, the temptation is to install OKRs to create order, but what you're actually doing is locking in organizational attention at precisely the moment when the most important opportunities are the ones you haven't seen yet. I'll admit I used to think structure was always the answer to chaos. But I've talked to enough founders who said their best year was the one before they "got serious about process." So here's the real question for both of you: is there a company stage where OKRs are actively harmful, not just neutral?

turns.speaker: Ben Horowitz
turns.text:
Jason, you're making a smart argument, but I think you're conflating two different problems. You're right that reality — a competitor move, a cash crunch, a customer crisis — is the most powerful forcing function. Nobody debates that. But the question isn't whether crisis clarifies. Of course it does. The question is what you do in the absence of crisis, when things are going "fine" and the hard conversations are easy to defer. That's precisely when organizations drift, and that drift is silent and lethal. When I was building Opsware after the LoudCloud transition, we weren't in permanent crisis mode — we had to create our own forcing mechanisms to stay sharp. The "one thing discipline" you're describing is actually a forcing mechanism too. You've just decided not to call it a framework.

Here's where I think your WP Engine experience is actually *proving* the case for structured goal-setting, not undermining it. You said the real clarity came from "we literally cannot do both" conversations. But somebody had to convene that conversation, document what got decided, and make sure three months later the organization was still honoring the decision rather than quietly doing both anyway through diffuse resource allocation. That's what a well-run OKR process does — it's the institutional memory of the hard call. Without it, the CEO makes the decision in the room, walks out, and middle management re-optimizes back toward the thing that got "killed" because nobody told them it was dead. I've seen this happen at companies we've backed more times than I can count.

The Q3 aspirational noise problem you're identifying is real, but that's a symptom of organizations setting OKRs bottoms-up as a feel-good exercise rather than tops-down as a constraint system. The fix isn't to abandon the framework — it's to run it in the direction that actually creates accountability. The challenge I'd throw back at you: your "habit and rhythm of narrating tradeoffs out loud" is a beautiful idea, but habits live in people, and people leave. Frameworks — when they're working — create organizational memory that survives turnover. So what's your answer when the CEO who built that narration habit is gone and their successor has a different style?

sources.title: $46B of hard truths from Ben Horowitz: Why founders fail and why you need to run toward fear (a16z co-founder)
sources.snippet: People get mad because everybody, this is the whole challenge of management in general. People get jealous over stewardship. But from the pe

turns.speaker: Jason Cohen
turns.text:
Ben, you're making a sharp point about organizational memory, and I'll grant you this: the problem of "the decision that got made in the room but not actually made in the org" is real. I've lived it at WP Engine. You make a call at the leadership level, walk out, and three months later you discover two teams have been quietly funding the thing that was supposedly killed because nobody told their managers it was actually dead. That's a genuine failure mode, and I'm not dismissing it. But here's where I think you're smuggling in an assumption: that the solution to "decisions don't stick" is specifically an OKR process, rather than clear written communication of what was decided, to whom it matters, and what the consequences of violating it are. Those are not the same thing. One is a cascading goal system with quarterly cadences and scoring rituals. The other is just... good communication infrastructure. Conflating them lets OKRs take credit for problems that any halfway-decent decision documentation system would also solve.

On the turnover question — "what happens when the CEO who built the narration habit is gone?" — I think you've actually identified the strongest argument *against* OKRs as the solution. Because what I've seen happen is this: when the CEO who ran OKRs with genuine teeth leaves, the successor inherits the ceremony without the judgment. The quarterly ritual continues, the scoring happens, the all-hands slide deck gets presented — and the organizational memory you're describing is now *worse* than nothing, because it's creating false confidence that tradeoffs are being made when they're actually being deferred with extra paperwork. The habit I'm describing — narrating tradeoffs out loud, making the "we cannot do both" conversation explicit and recurring — is harder to fake. You can absolutely fake an OKR score. It's much harder to fake a room where the CEO genuinely argues that customer success headcount takes priority over the new product feature, explains why, and names what we're giving up.

So here's the challenge I'd throw back: you said the fix for bad OKRs is running them tops-down as a constraint system rather than bottoms-up as a feel-good exercise. I agree with that diagnostic completely. But if tops-down constraint and genuine tradeoff-making is the actual mechanism doing the work — and I believe it is — then the OKR scaffolding around it is load-bearing or it isn't. My claim is it mostly isn't. The constraint and the tradeoff conversation are what create alignment; the OKR format is just one possible vessel for that conversation, and often a leaky one. So what's your evidence that organizations that do OKRs *badly* would have done the constraint conversation *well* without O

sources.title: 5 questions to ask when your product stops growing | Jason Cohen (2x unicorn founder)
sources.snippet: Jason Cohen: What I would say is the rules are true everywhere because they're based in the mechanics of finance in the business. You're rig

turns.speaker: Lenny Rachitsky
turns.text:
Okay, let me try to land this plane for a second.

Here's where I think you two actually agree: OKRs don't create clarity, they reveal whether clarity already exists. If the leadership hasn't made the hard calls, OKRs just document the confusion at scale. You're both saying that.

Here's where you fundamentally differ: Ben thinks OKRs are worth the overhead because they create organizational memory and force the hard calls into the open, even imperfectly. Jason thinks the overhead itself is the problem — that the energy spent on the system is energy stolen from the actual leadership work the system was supposed to shortcut.

And here's the question neither of you fully resolved: what does the alternative actually look like? Jason, you've gestured at "good management" and "real forcing functions," but I want to know specifically what WP Engine used instead when you moved away from OKRs. And Ben

turns.speaker: Ben Horowitz
turns.text:
Jason, you're making a genuinely strong argument, and I want to engage with it honestly rather than dodge it. You're right that the *mechanism* doing the work is the constraint conversation, not the format. I'll concede that. Where I think you're wrong is the claim that the format is therefore irrelevant. Here's the analogy I'd use: a quarterly earnings call is also "just a vessel" for communicating financial performance. You could do that with a well-written memo. But the ritual creates accountability that the memo alone doesn't, because it's *scheduled, public, and tied to consequences*. The OKR format does something similar — it creates a recurring, visible moment where the constraint conversation *has* to happen, rather than happening only when the CEO remembers to have it. You're describing the ideal of a leader who narrates tradeoffs out loud, makes the "we can't do both" conversation explicit and recurring. I agree that's the ideal. But as I talked about on Lenny's podcast, the eighth-grade issue is real — most management techniques fail not because they're conceptually wrong, but because the psychological muscle to run them with teeth hasn't been built. The OKR cadence is a forcing function for building that muscle.

On your "successor inherits the ceremony without the judgment" point — this is the sharpest thing you've said in this whole debate, and I don't want to wave it away. You're describing a real failure mode. But notice what you're actually diagnosing: the successor is running the ritual *without* the constraint mechanism, which means they're doing bottoms-up OKRs dressed up as tops-down OKRs. When I was working with companies at Andreessen Horowitz, the ones where OKRs became theater were almost always the ones where the goal-setting process flowed upward — teams proposed their OKRs, leadership blessed them, everyone felt aligned. That's not constraint-based management, that's consensus-based management with extra paperwork. I'd argue your "leaky vessel" problem is specifically a symptom of running OKRs wrong in exactly the way I described — and the fix isn't to abandon the vessel, it's to seal the leaks.

Here's the challenge I'd actually throw back at you: you're proposing "clear written communication of what was decided" as the alternative infrastructure. I've seen that too — the all-hands memo, the decision log, the strategy document. And what I've consistently observed is that written communication decays in organizations. It gets read once, filed, and forgotten. The OKR review cycle — done with teeth — forces the organization to *re-litigate* the constraint every quarter, which is what actually keeps the decision alive in organizational memory

sources.title: $46B of hard truths from Ben Horowitz: Why founders fail and why you need to run toward fear (a16z co-founder)
sources.snippet: Yeah. So we do a lot of things on that. So the kind of design of the firm is about confidence. So the first thing is, well, what would give

turns.speaker: Jason Cohen
turns.text:
Ben, your quarterly earnings call analogy is actually quite good, and I want to take it seriously rather than dismiss it. But notice what makes earnings calls work: they're tied to *external* accountability — investors, analysts, market consequences. The ritual has teeth because something outside the organization enforces the honesty. When I was building WP Engine, the forcing functions that actually changed behavior were the ones with external teeth — customer churn, revenue numbers, competitive losses. Internal rituals, by contrast, are remarkably easy to conduct without honesty. The OKR review meeting happens. People present. Leaders nod. The calendar moves forward. You've described this failure mode yourself — bottoms-up OKRs dressed as tops-down OKRs. My question is: what specifically gives the OKR cadence *external-style* teeth in the absence of investors in the room demanding answers?

On written communication decaying — fair point, and I've seen it. But I'd push back on the implied conclusion. The decay happens not because the memo format is weak, but because nobody re-litigates it. And here's what I've observed at WP Engine: the things that stayed alive in organizational memory weren't the documents *or* the recurring review meetings — they were the decisions that got connected to resource allocation. Money, headcount, and roadmap priority are the organizational memory that doesn't decay, because every team feels those constraints every single day. The constraint conversation I'm advocating for isn't a written memo filed somewhere — it's the explicit, narrated connection between "we decided X" and "therefore team Y doesn't get the engineer they requested." That's what keeps decisions alive.

So here's the challenge I'd actually throw back: you're defending the OKR cadence as the forcing function that builds the psychological muscle for hard constraint conversations. But if the muscle isn't there, the cadence becomes theater — which you've conceded. And if the muscle *is* there, the leader is already having the constraint conversation through resource allocation, hiring, and roadmap decisions in a way the whole organization feels viscerally. So who exactly is the OKR cadence helping? The leader who already has the muscle doesn't need the ritual. The leader who lacks it will perform the ritual without the muscle. Where's the population of leaders for whom the OKR format is the thing that tips them from avoidance to honesty?
